activities_projects.title: Mapping the Sial and Sima
activities_projects.objective: Compare the composition and thickness of continental and oceanic crust
steps: Cut a thick brown strip (35 km) for Continental Crust and label it 'SIAL' (Silica + Alumina)
Cut a thinner blue strip (5 km) for Oceanic Crust and label it 'SIMA' (Silica + Magnesium)
Place the blue strip slightly lower to show the ocean floor
Write the primary minerals found in each on the back of the strips
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Distinguishes between the two types of crust based on mineralogy and depth
activities_projects.evaluation: Accurate representation of thickness ratios and mineral components (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Magma vs. Lava: Cooling Rates
activities_projects.objective: Understand the difference between Intrusive and Extrusive Igneous rocks
steps: Dissolve Epsom salt in hot water until saturated
Pour half into Jar A and leave it at room temperature (slow cooling like Magma/Granite)
Pour half into Jar B and place it in the fridge (fast cooling like Lava/Basalt)
Observe the crystal size in both after 24 hours
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Explains how cooling speed affects the grain size of igneous rocks
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct identification of large vs. small crystals in relation to cooling (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Stratified Sedimentary Model
activities_projects.objective: Simulate the formation of sedimentary layers over time
steps: Fill the bottle with water and add a handful of each material
Shake the bottle vigorously to simulate a turbulent river
Let it stand undisturbed for 2 hours
Observe how the materials settle into distinct layers (strata)
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Demonstrates the settling of sediments and the layered nature of sedimentary rocks
activities_projects.evaluation: Logical explanation of why heavier materials settle at the bottom (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Pressure Cooker: Metamorphism
activities_projects.objective: Understand how heat and pressure change rock properties
steps: Make small balls of different colored clay representing minerals in a rock
Stack them together loosely to represent a sedimentary rock
Place heavy books on top and apply pressure while rolling the clay in your warm hands
Observe how the layers flatten and merge into a new, harder texture
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Visualizes the transformation of rocks into metamorphic forms without melting
activities_projects.evaluation: Ability to relate the physical change in clay to the formation of Slate or Marble (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Rock Cycle Flow Analysis
activities_projects.objective: Map the transitions between the three rock types
steps: Draw three circles for Igneous, Sedimentary, and Metamorphic rocks
Place the correct keyword on the arrow connecting them (e.g., Igneous to Sedimentary = Weathering)
Add an extra loop showing how any rock can return to Magma through melting
Identify where 'Lithification' occurs in the cycle
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Synthesizes the dynamic and continuous nature of rock transformation
activities_projects.evaluation: Technical accuracy of all transition processes in the cycle (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Nife: The Core's Secret
activities_projects.objective: Investigate the properties of the Earth's innermost layer
materials: Nickel (if available) or heavy metallic objects
steps: Research why the core is called 'NIFE' (Nickel + Iron)
Test the magnetic properties of iron and observe its density/weight
Discuss why the core has extremely high temperature and pressure
Explain how this metallic core contributes to Earth's magnetic field
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Identifies the chemical composition and extreme conditions of the Core
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct identification of minerals and their physical properties in the core (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Architecture and Rocks
activities_projects.objective: Identify the use of specific rocks in historical and modern buildings
steps: Find out which rocks were used to build the Red Fort and the Taj Mahal
Identify if they are Igneous, Sedimentary, or Metamorphic
Explain why Marble is used for carvings while Granite is used for floors/kitchen tops
List the properties that make these rocks suitable for construction
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Relates the physical properties of rocks to their human utility
activities_projects.evaluation: Accuracy in identifying building materials and their rock types (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Fossil Record Hunt
activities_projects.objective: Analyze why fossils are exclusive to specific rock types
steps: Place 'gummy worms' between slices of bread and press firmly
Observe the flattened 'fossil' in the layers
Discuss why fossils are destroyed in the 'melting' of igneous rocks or the 'heat' of metamorphic rocks
Conclude why they are only preserved in sedimentary strata
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Explains the environmental conditions necessary for fossil preservation
activities_projects.evaluation: Scientific reasoning for the absence of fossils in igneous/metamorphic rocks (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Mineral Identification Lab
steps: Observe a piece of Granite closely; identify the different colored specks in it
Identify those specks as minerals like Quartz, Feldspar, and Mica
Write a definition for a 'Rock' as an aggregate of minerals
List 3 industrial uses of these minerals
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands that rocks are composed of naturally occurring minerals
activities_projects.evaluation: Clarity in defining the relationship between rocks and minerals (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Earth's Interior Cross-Section Quiz
activities_projects.objective: Recall depth and temperature facts of the Earth's layers
steps: Label the Crust (0-35km), Mantle (2900km), and Core (3500km radius)
Identify the 'Lithosphere' as the crust and uppermost mantle
Mark the temperature trend (increasing towards the center)
Write down the percentage of Earth's volume each layer occupies (1%, 84%, 15%)
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Memorizes key quantitative data regarding Earth's internal structure
activities_projects.evaluation: Precision in labeling depths and volume percentages (10 marks)
